title: Pumpernickel
subtitle: Dense Westphalian rye bread baked low and slow
excerpt: A small, very dense loaf made from rye flour and soaked cracked rye, baked covered at low heat for two and a half hours. The long bake darkens the natural rye sugars to near-black and develops the characteristic slightly sweet, earthy flavour. Slices thin and keeps for a week.
sourceNotes: Pumpernickel is one of the oldest documented German breads, with references in Westphalian provincial cookery from the sixteenth century onwards. Methods appear in Henriette Davidis, Praktisches Kochbuch (1844, public domain), which gives the long low-temperature covered bake as the defining technique. Cross-referenced with early-twentieth-century German household encyclopaedias available through digitised German library archives.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice thinly and freeze in portions. Thaw at room temperature for 15 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cracked rye needs to soak overnight. Bake the following day. The loaf improves when wrapped and rested for a day before cutting.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, covered tin. Conventional 140°C. Cover the tin with its lid or a tight layer of foil for the first 2 hours, then uncover for the final 30 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pumpernickel is baked covered at a very low temperature for 2 to 3 hours rather than the 30 to 45 minutes typical of a wheat loaf. The steam trapped inside the covered tin keeps the dough from drying out while the temperature slowly caramelises the natural sugars in the rye flour through the Maillard reaction, turning the crumb near-black and giving the loaf its characteristic sweet, slightly bitter depth. Authentic Westphalian pumpernickel uses whole rye berries and sourdough; this version uses cracked rye and fast-action yeast for a simpler result.

content.text: Pumpernickel is a rye bread that owes its near-black colour and complex flavour to time rather than technique: a covered tin at very low heat, left in the oven for two and a half hours. This is the
adapted for a domestic oven with fast-action yeast. The cracked rye needs an overnight soak, so start the evening before. One small loaf, serving 10 to 12 slices.

items.prepNote: soaked overnight in 150 ml cold water

content.text: Combine the cracked rye with 150 ml of cold water in a small bowl. Cover and leave at room temperature overnight. The grains will absorb most of the water by morning.

The following day, combine the rye flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Add the treacle, the soaked cracked rye with any remaining soaking water, and 160 ml of warm water. Mix thoroughly into a dense, sticky dough; rye dough does not develop gluten the way wheat dough does, so it will not be smooth. Cover and leave in a warm place for 90 minutes.

Grease the loaf tin well. Wet your hands and press the dough into the tin, smoothing the top as flat as possible. Cover with cling film and leave for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 130°C fan (140°C conventional) during this time.

Cover the tin tightly with its lid or with a double layer of foil. Bake for 2 hours covered, then remove the lid or foil and bake for a further 30 minutes. The loaf will darken to deep brown, pull away slightly from the sides of the tin, and feel firm when pressed. Turn out onto a wire rack and cool completely before wrapping. Rest for at least one day before cutting; the flavour and texture improve significantly overnight.
